title: Meet LUCA: The Great-Great-Great-Great… Grandparent of Every Living Thing!
summary:
Have you ever wondered who your oldest ancestor is? Not your great-grandpa — we're talking way, way older. Like, billions of years older!

Scientists call it LUCA. That stands for Last Universal Common Ancestor. LUCA wasn't the first living thing on Earth, but it's special because every single living thing today — you, your dog, the mushrooms on your pizza, and even the bacteria in your belly — is related to it. We're all distant cousins!

So what did LUCA look like? Scientists think it was a single cell, probably shaped like a rod (like a tiny hot dog). It probably lived near a hydrothermal vent at the bottom of the ocean — that's like an underwater volcano that spews hot, mineral-rich water.

A team of scientists led by Tom Williams from the University of Bath recently figured out more about LUCA than ever before. They think LUCA lived about 4 billion years ago. It ate, it had insides, and it was a microbe.

Why do scientists care? Well, understanding LUCA helps us understand how life evolved on Earth. And that might help us look for life on other planets! Plus, it's just cool to know where we all came from.

"If you want to understand the early evolution of life, then you're talking about events that happened to microbes," says Williams. "I think that is quite important."

So next time you see a tree or a bird, remember: you're related! Thanks, LUCA.
why_it_matters: You share a common ancestor with all living things – trees, mushrooms, bacteria – and that means we are all connected in one big family tree.

keywords.explanation: Last Universal Common Ancestor, the shared ancestor of all life today
LUCA was a tiny living thing made of just one cell
An underwater volcano that spews hot water, where LUCA likely lived
The scientist who led the team studying LUCA

options: Near a hydrothermal vent at the bottom of the ocean

background_read: Scientists think LUCA lived about 4 billion years ago, when Earth was very young.
Before LUCA, there were probably other simple life forms, but they died out.
Understanding LUCA helps scientists look for life on other planets.
Article_Structure: WHO: Tom Williams and his team from the University of Bath and University of Barcelona
WHAT: Figured out more details about LUCA, the last common ancestor of all life
WHERE: At the University of Bath and University of Barcelona
WHY: To understand how life evolved on Earth and help find life on other planets

perspectives.description: They now have a clearer picture of what LUCA was like, which helps them study the origins of life.
It's amazing to think that every living thing is related, and it makes you look at nature differently.
Some wonder how we can know so much about something that lived so long ago, but science keeps finding clues.
